perturbation.original_text: "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line). Customer's license to use the Software shall be limited to, and Customer shall not use the Software in excess of, a single hardware chassis or card or that number of agent(s), concurrent users, sessions, IP addresses, port(s), seat(s), server(s) or site(s), as set forth in the applicable Purchase Order which has been accepted by Cisco and for which Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee.
perturbation.changed_text: "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software. Customer's license to use the Software shall be limited to, and Customer shall not use the Software in excess of, a single hardware chassis or card or that number of agent(s), concurrent users, sessions, IP addresses, port(s), seat(s), server(s) or site(s), as set forth in the applicable Purchase Order regardless of whether it has been accepted by Cisco and regardless of whether Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line)' from the definition of 'Documentation', we introduce uncertainty as to whether the documentation needs to be provided by Cisco to be considered 'Documentation' under the agreement. By removing 'which has been accepted by Cisco and for which Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee', a contradiction is being made on weather the 'Purchase Order' need to be accepted by 'Cisco' and if it needs to be 'paid for' by the customer.
perturbation.contradicted_text: License. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Cisco Systems, Inc. or its subsidiary licensing the Software instead of Cisco Systems, Inc. ("Cisco"), grants to Customer a nonexclusive and nontransferable license to use for Customer's internal business purposes the Software and the Documentation for which Customer has paid the required license fees. "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software. Customer's license to use the Software shall be limited to, and Customer shall not use the Software in excess of, a single hardware chassis or card or that number of agent(s), concurrent users, sessions, IP addresses, port(s), seat(s), server(s) or site(s), as set forth in the applicable Purchase Order regardless of whether it has been accepted by Cisco and regardless of whether Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee. Unless otherwise expressly provided in the Documentation, Customer shall use the Software solely as embedded in, for execution on, or (where the applicable documentation permits installation on non-Cisco equipment) for communication with Cisco equipment owned or leased by Customer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement that the Purchase Order be accepted by Cisco and that the Customer has paid the required license fee. This directly contradicts the 'License' section of the Contradicted Text, which states the license is granted for Software and Documentation 'for which Customer has paid the required license fees.' Additionally, it contradicts the implicit requirement of Cisco's acceptance for a valid Purchase Order. This change introduces a strong contradiction regarding the conditions under which the license is valid, creating potential unenforceability and disputes.

perturbation.original_text: General Limitations. Except as otherwise expressly provided under this Agreement, Customer shall have no right, and Customer specifically agrees not to: (i) transfer, assign or sublicense its license rights to any other person or entity, or use the Software on unauthorized or secondhand Cisco equipment, and Customer acknowledges that any attempted transfer, assignment, sublicense or use shall be void; (ii) make error corrections to or otherwise modify or adapt the Software or create derivative works based upon the Software, or permit third parties to do the same; (iii) decompile, decrypt, reverse engineer, disassemble or otherwise reduce the Software to human- readable form; or (iv) use or permit the Software to be used to perform services for third parties without the express written authorization of Cisco.
perturbation.changed_text: General Limitations. Except as otherwise expressly provided under this Agreement, Customer shall have the right, and Customer agrees to: (i) transfer, assign or sublicense its license rights to any other person or entity, or use the Software on unauthorized or secondhand Cisco equipment, and Customer acknowledges that any attempted transfer, assignment, sublicense or use shall be valid; (ii) make error corrections to or otherwise modify or adapt the Software or create derivative works based upon the Software, or permit third parties to do the same; (iii) decompile, decrypt, reverse engineer, disassemble or otherwise reduce the Software to human- readable form; or (iv) use or permit the Software to be used to perform services for third parties without the express written authorization of Cisco.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall have no right' to 'shall have the right' and 'agrees not to' to 'agrees to', and 'shall be void' to 'shall be valid', the customer is now in power to transfer, assign or sublicense its license rights to any other person or entity, or use the Software on unauthorized or secondhand Cisco equipment, and Customer acknowledges that any attempted transfer, assignment, sublicense or use shall be valid. This creates a direct contradiction with the clause's intention. This impacts the Proprietary Notices section, allowing the customer to disclose the software.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Protection of Information. Customer agrees that aspects of the Software and associated Documentation, including the specific design and structure of individual programs, are trade secrets and/or copyrighted materials of Cisco, its suppliers or licensors. Customer shall not disclose, provide, or otherwise make available such trade secrets or copyrighted material in any form to any third party without the prior written consent of Cisco. Customer shall implement reasonable security measures to protect such trade secrets and copyrighted materials. Title to Software and Documentation shall remain solely with Cisco, its suppliers or licensors.
perturbation.justification: The changed text grants the Customer the right to transfer, assign, or sublicense license rights, directly contradicting the 'Protection of Information' section. This section explicitly states that the Customer shall not disclose or make available trade secrets or copyrighted material to any third party without Cisco's prior written consent. Allowing the customer to transfer license rights inherently permits the disclosure of the Software and Documentation, creating a direct and impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This limited warranty extends only to the Customer who is the original licensee. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this limited warranty will be, at Cisco's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco.
perturbation.changed_text: This limited warranty extends to any Customer. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this limited warranty will be, at the Customer's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'This limited warranty extends only to the Customer who is the original licensee' to 'This limited warranty extends to any Customer', and changing 'at Cisco's option' to 'at the Customer's option', this makes the warranty applicable to any customer and not just the original customer, and the 'repair, replacement, or refund of the software' now resides on the customer, this creates a contradiction regarding who is liable. This also contradicts the 'Disclaimer of Liabilities' Section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: DISCLAIMER OF LIABILITIES. IN NO EVENT WILL CISCO OR ITS SUPPLIERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY LOST REVENUE, PROFIT, OR LOST OR DAMAGED DATA, OR FOR SPECIAL, INDIRECT, CONSEQUENTIAL, INCIDENTAL, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES HOWEVER CAUSED AND REGARDLESS OF THE THEORY OF LIABILITY OR WHETHER ARISING OUT OF THE USE OF OR INABILITY TO USE SOFTWARE OR OTHERWISE AND EVEN IF CISCO OR ITS SUPPLIERS OR LICENSORS HAVE BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. In no event shall Cisco's or its suppliers' or licensors' liability to Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise, exceed the price paid by Customer for the Software that gave rise to the claim or if the Software is part of another Product, the price paid for such other Product. The foregoing limitations shall apply even if the above stated warranty fails of its essential purpose. BECAUSE SOME STATES OR JURISDICTIONS DO NOT ALLOW LIMITATION OR EXCLUSION OF CONSEQUENTIAL OR INCIDENTAL DAMAGES, THE ABOVE LIMITATION MAY NOT APPLY TO YOU.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly alters the scope of the warranty and who gets to make the decision, creating a direct conflict with the 'DISCLAIMER OF LIABILITIES' section. The original text limits the warranty to the original licensee and gives Cisco the option of repair/replacement/refund, while the changed text extends the warranty to any Customer and gives that option to the Customer. This directly contradicts the clause that limits Cisco's liability and places the decision of whether to repair, replace, or refund the software on the customer.
